courses.title: Engineering Mathematics III — Differential Equations & Linear Algebra
courses.summary: The mathematics of dynamic systems: first- and second-order differential equations, Laplace transforms, and the matrix language of coupled systems — the toolset vibrations, controls, and heat transfer stand on.

lessons.t: First-order equations revisited: separable and linear
lessons.scope: Separable equations recapped from the calculus course, then the linear first-order equation solved with the integrating factor, derived rather than decreed. Solution families and the initial condition that picks one. The course's working vocabulary set in one lesson.
preview: Solve y' + 2y = 6 with y(0) = 0: what value does y approach, and how fast? (3, with τ = 0.5)
What exactly does the integrating factor multiply away?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 3 — Solving First-order Linear ODEs

lessons.scope: Mixing tanks, thermal soak, and RC circuits: one balance statement each, and the same equation three times. Time constants read directly from coefficients. Steady state as the equation's destination, transient as its journey.
preview: Write the ODE for a 5 m³ well-mixed tank flushed at 0.25 m³/min, and read off τ. (20 min)
Why do a cooling casting and a discharging capacitor share one mathematics?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 1 — The Geometrical View of y' = f(x,y)

lessons.scope: The characteristic equation turns a differential equation into algebra. Overdamped, critically damped, and underdamped responses classified by the discriminant. Why the damping ratio will become the vibration engineer's favorite number.
preview: Classify y'' + 4y' + 3y = 0 by its roots. (−1 and −3: overdamped)
What does a complex-conjugate root pair mean physically?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 9 — Second-order Linear ODEs with Constant Coefficients

lessons.scope: Undetermined coefficients for constant, ramp, and sinusoidal forcing. The structure theorem: general solution = homogeneous + particular. Transient versus steady-state response — the vocabulary the controls course inherits whole.
preview: Why does the transient die out while the forced response persists?
What particular-solution form do you try for a constant force input?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 13 — Finding Particular Solutions to Inhomogeneous ODEs

lessons.scope: The mass–spring–damper equation solved end to end. Resonance: why amplitude grows when forcing approaches the natural frequency, and why machines care at one-times running speed. Beats in brief.
preview: Compute ωn for m = 4 kg, k = 10 kN/m. (50 rad/s)
What limits the resonant amplitude in a real machine?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 14 — Resonance (reinforcement/bridge lecture)

lessons.scope: The transform defined and a working table built by direct integration. Transforms of derivatives: calculus becomes algebra, and initial conditions walk in automatically. Linearity as the property that makes the method industrial.
preview: What is the transform of e^(−3t)? (1/(s+3))
How do initial conditions enter a transformed equation without extra work?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 19 — Introduction to the Laplace Transform (reinforcement/bridge lecture)

lessons.scope: Inverse transforms by partial fractions; step inputs and switched forcing handled cleanly. Initial-value problems solved entirely in the s-domain. The transfer-function idea planted for the controls course.
preview: What does dividing by s do to a signal in the time domain?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 22 — Laplace Transforms with Discontinuous Inputs

lessons.t: Systems of equations: the matrix view
lessons.scope: Coupled tanks and coupled masses written as x' = Ax. Why coupled dynamics needs matrices rather than more calculus. The eigenvalue preview: a system's tempo hides in A itself.
preview: Write the two-tank mixing problem as a matrix equation.
What plays the characteristic equation's role for a system of equations?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 24 — Introduction to First-order Systems of ODEs

lessons.scope: Gaussian elimination, rank, and solvability; determinants at working strength. The numerical caution: ill-conditioned systems amplify data noise, and engineering data is never clean. Where matrix solving hides inside every simulation tool.
preview: Solve a 3×3 system by elimination, tracking each pivot.
What does a near-zero determinant warn you about the answer?

video.title: MIT 18.02 Lec 4 — Square Systems

lessons.scope: Eigenvalues computed for 2×2 and 3×3 matrices; eigenvalues as the system's tempo — decay rates and oscillation frequencies — and eigenvectors as its mode shapes. The modal language of the vibrations course is born here.
preview: Find the eigenvalues of [[2,1],[1,2]]. (1 and 3)
Why do the signs of eigenvalues decide stability?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 25 — Homogeneous Linear Systems (eigenvalues at work)

lessons.scope: Euler's method and Runge–Kutta; step size traded against accuracy and stability, observed live on the cooling equation. When numerical solution is the only honest route — and how to distrust it productively. Closes the loop with the computing course.
preview: Take two Euler steps of y' = −y, y(0) = 1, h = 0.5. (0.25)
Why can too large a step make a stable equation explode numerically?

video.title: MIT 18.03 Lec 2 — Euler's Numerical Method

courses.career:
Differential equations are the native language of every engineer who predicts how a system behaves over time — the sector only changes the system:

  • Automotive & heavy equipment — ride and driveline engineers at manufacturers such as Toyota tune suspensions and mounts as mass–spring–damper systems, reading \( \zeta \) and \( \omega_n \) straight off this course to decide whether a chassis slams, settles, or rings.
  • Aerospace & defense — flight-control and structures engineers at houses such as Airbus live in second-order dynamics and Laplace transforms: every actuator loop and flutter margin is the forced-response and eigenvalue mathematics of Lessons 4–10.
  • Industrial technology & controls — automation engineers at suppliers such as Rockwell Automation size every servo, filter, and PID loop from time constants and pole locations — the state-space and eigenvalue view of Lessons 8–10 is their daily model.

Interview angle: the recurring probe is qualitative — "sketch the response of this system without solving it." The discriminant, \( \omega_n \), and \( \zeta \) drilled here are the expected instant answer, before any integration.

courses.title: Strength of Materials
courses.summary: Stress where it lives: axial members, torsion, bending, combined loading, and failure theories — the course that turns statics diagrams into the numbers written on repair procedures and fitness-for-service notes.

lessons.t: Stress and strain under axial load
lessons.scope: Normal stress and strain defined from first principles; the tensile curve reread as an engineer's contract — modulus, yield, ultimate, ductility. Allowable stress and factor of safety as institutional honesty. Saint-Venant's caution about where formulas apply.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 6; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: A 16 mm bar carries 45 kN: compute the stress and the safety factor against 355 MPa yield.
Engineering versus true stress: which keeps rising after necking, and why?
Why do aluminum alloys need the 0.2% offset construction to define yield at all?

video.title: MoM Lesson 9 — The Stress-Strain Diagram

lessons.scope: Deformation of stepped and composite bars. Statically indeterminate axial problems. Thermal expansion against restraint — the piping stress problem in embryo.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: Two bars in series under one load: which stretches more and why?
Why does restrained thermal expansion create stress without any external load?

video.title: MoM Lesson 21 — Thermal Expansion and Axial Elongation

lessons.scope: Shear stress and angle of twist in circular shafts derived. Power–torque–speed relations for drivelines. Sizing a pump shaft and reading a torsional failure surface's 45° signature.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: Why does a torsional fatigue crack spiral at 45°?
Double a shaft's diameter: what happens to its torsional stress at the same torque?

video.title: MoM Lesson 23 — Shear Stress Due to Torsion, Polar Moment

lessons.t: Bending and transverse shear in beams
lessons.scope: The flexure formula derived from plane sections; section modulus as the designer's currency. Shear stress distribution and shear flow — why webs carry shear and flanges carry moment in an I-beam. Built-up sections and their fastener spacing, and where beams actually fail.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: Why does bending stress vanish at the neutral axis and peak at the surface?
Why do I-beam webs carry the shear while flanges carry the moment?

video.title: MoM Lesson 31 — The Flexure Formula

lessons.scope: Elastic curve by integration; superposition with standard cases. Stiffness as a serviceability limit distinct from strength. Machinery baseplate and walkway deflection checks.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: Stiffness and strength are different failures: give a case where deflection governs.
Why does superposition work for beam deflections?

video.title: MoM Lesson 62 — Slope and Deflection Introduction

lessons.scope: Axial, torsion, bending, and pressure superposed at a point. Critical-point hunting on real components. A shaft with a pulley worked completely — the pattern for machine design.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: At a shaft surface point under bending plus torsion: which stresses combine, and how?
Why is the critical point rarely obvious before you check candidates?

video.title: MoM Lesson 44 — Combined Loading Introduction

lessons.scope: Plane-stress transformation derived, then Mohr's circle as its graphical engine. Principal stresses and maximum shear located. Why fatigue cracks pick their planes.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: What do Mohr's circle's center and radius each represent?
Why do principal planes carry no shear?

video.title: MoM Lesson 50 — Mohr's Circle for Stress Transformation

lessons.scope: Hoop and axial stress derived from equilibrium. The 2:1 hoop-to-axial ratio and what it predicts about vessel failure orientation. First contact with the equipment class Asset Integrity will govern.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: Derive the 2:1 hoop-to-axial ratio and say which way a vessel should split first.
When does thin-wall theory stop being honest?

video.title: MoM Lesson 41 — Intro to Thin-Walled Pressure Vessels

lessons.scope: Tresca and von Mises for ductile metals; maximum normal stress for brittle ones. Using them on combined-stress states from lesson 7. What 'the stress' means when there are six components.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: Why does von Mises predict yielding better than max-normal-stress for ductile metals?
A pure-shear state: what does each theory predict for yield?

video.title: MoM Lesson 55 — Tresca, Von Mises, and Rankine Failure Theories

lessons.scope: Euler buckling derived; effective length and slenderness. The crossover between yielding and buckling. Why long slender compression members fail sideways without warning.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: Why does buckling depend on stiffness and length but not strength?
What does effective length encode about end conditions?

video.title: MoM Lesson 66 — Intro to Column Buckling

lessons.scope: Kt at holes, fillets, and keyways; the geometry tax on nominal stress. Notch sensitivity in brief. The handshake with Materials II's fatigue lesson — geometry and metallurgy jointly set life.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 8; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech) — mechanics of materials
preview: Why does a keyway multiply nominal stress, and by roughly how much order-wise?
What does notch sensitivity add to the geometric Kt story?

video.title: MoM Lesson 22 — Stress Concentration (Stress Risers)

courses.career:
Strength of Materials is the sizing calculation behind every load-bearing part — the sector picks which parts:

  • Construction & heavy structures — structural engineers at contractors such as Bechtel size beams and columns by the section modulus and allowable-stress logic of this course; the flexure formula is their daily tool.
  • Oil, gas & process plant — piping and pressure engineers at operators such as Shell live in the thermal-stress and restraint problem of Lesson 2 — expansion loops and supports exist to keep \( E\alpha\Delta T \) below allowable.
  • Automotive & rotating machinery — driveline and machine engineers at makers such as Caterpillar size shafts by \( \tau_{max}=16T/\pi d^3 \) and read torsional fracture surfaces exactly as Lesson 3 teaches.

Interview angle: the classic probe is "size this shaft/beam for this load" — the allowable-stress inversion (\( S\ge M/\sigma_{allow} \), \( d=(16T/\pi\tau)^{1/3} \)) is the expected working answer, with the factor of safety stated explicitly.

courses.title: Fluid Mechanics
courses.summary: Pressure, flow, and loss: statics to Bernoulli to real pipe networks with pumps attached — taught so that a pump curve, a system curve, and an NPSH margin become native reading.
taught_from: Çengel & Cimbala, Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and Applications (McGraw-Hill)

lessons.scope: Density, viscosity, vapor pressure, surface tension — each tied to a plant consequence. Newtonian behavior and where slurries and polymers depart from it. Vapor pressure flagged early: cavitation is coming.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 2; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: Why does vapor pressure, not boiling point, decide cavitation risk?
Newtonian or not: what does the label actually claim?

video.title: Understanding Viscosity

alternatives.title: Understanding Viscosity of Fluids: Theory & Experiments
Understanding Viscosity and Viscous Force
Understanding Viscosity in Fluids

lessons.scope: Pressure fields, manometry, and forces on submerged surfaces. Gauge versus absolute pressure — the eternal instrumentation trap. Tank and dam wall calculations done cleanly.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 3; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: Gauge versus absolute: which does a transmitter read, and when does it bite?
Why does force on a submerged gate act below its centroid?

video.title: Fluid/Hydrostatic Pressure in 11 Minutes!

alternatives.title: Hydrostatic Pressure in 8 Minutes!
Hydrostatic Pressure (Fluid Mechanics Lesson 3)
Hydrostatic Pressure Basics

lessons.t: Flow description, Reynolds number, and continuity
lessons.scope: Streamlines and flow regimes; the Reynolds number as the master dimensionless group, with laminar and turbulent flow distinguished physically and transition located in real pipe sizes. Control-volume mass balances, steady and unsteady, and velocity–area trades in ducts and nozzles. The one-line continuity equation that gating design already used in MFG 105.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 4–5, 8; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: Compute-style: what Reynolds number regime is water at 2 m/s in a 100 mm pipe?
Halve a duct's area at constant flow: what must velocity do?

video.title: Understanding Reynolds Number

alternatives.title: Reynolds Number — Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow in 8 Minutes
Reynolds Number Explained: Complete Engineering Guide
Laminar Flow, Turbulent Flow and Reynolds Number

lessons.t: Bernoulli, energy equation, pipe flow, and pump curves
lessons.scope: Bernoulli derived with its assumptions on the table, then upgraded to the energy equation with head loss and pump head. Major losses via friction factor and the Moody chart; minor losses by coefficient. System curve meets pump curve — the operating point found, the lesson this whole course exists to deliver.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 5, 8, 14; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: Static head 12 m plus 6 m friction at design flow: what pump head is needed, and what happens if friction doubles?
Why does the pump-curve/system-curve intersection define a stable operating point?
In turbulent pipe flow, head loss scales with roughly which power of velocity — and what does that mean for 'just open the valve more'?

video.title: Understanding Bernoulli's Equation

alternatives.title: Bernoulli's Equation & Flow in Pipes — Understanding Bernoulli
Pumps in Bernoulli's Equation
Flow with a Pump

lessons.scope: Control-volume momentum for forces on bends, nozzles, and vanes. Thrust blocks and pipe anchor loads. Why elbows in large lines need restraint calculations.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 6; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: Why does a pipe bend need an anchor even at steady flow?
What momentum change does a nozzle impose on its bracket?

alternatives.title: Conservation of Linear Momentum for a Control Volume

lessons.scope: Buckingham Pi with engineering judgment. Model testing and scale-up logic. The affinity-law preview: why pump performance scales the way it does.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 7; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: What does Buckingham Pi promise about model tests?
Why do pump affinity laws follow from similarity?

lessons.t: Laminar and turbulent pipe flow in depth
lessons.scope: Fully developed laminar flow derived to the Hagen–Poiseuille result. Turbulent velocity profiles and roughness effects. Entrance lengths and where gauges should not be installed.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 8; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: Where does Hagen–Poiseuille's fourth-power radius dependence come from?
Why do entrance lengths matter for meter placement?

alternatives.title: Laminar & Turbulent Pipe Flow, The Moody Diagram

Turbulent Flow: Moody Chart

lessons.scope: Series and parallel lines; three-reservoir style problems by iteration. Valve types and their loss and control characters. Reading a pump-and-piping isometric as a resistance network.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 8; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: Two parallel lines: how does flow split, physically?
What does a globe valve's high loss coefficient buy you?

lessons.scope: Orifice, venturi, rotameter, magnetic, ultrasonic, and Coriolis meters — principle, accuracy, and installation effects. Differential-pressure metering derived from Bernoulli. Straight-run requirements as physics, not superstition.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 8; instrumentation sections; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: Why does an orifice meter trade permanent loss for cheapness?
Which meter survives dirty slurry, and which demands clean fluid?

lessons.scope: Euler turbomachine equation derived from angular momentum. Velocity triangles for a centrifugal impeller. Specific speed as the shape parameter of the pump world.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, Ch. 14; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: What does the Euler equation say a pump impeller does to angular momentum?
What does specific speed classify?

lessons.scope: Mach number, choking, and converging-nozzle behavior. Relief and blowdown implications. Enough gas dynamics to respect what happens downstream of a PSV.
lessons.src: Çengel & Cimbala, compressible flow chapter; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder) — fluids
preview: What chokes at a nozzle throat, and at what Mach number?
Why does a PSV's downstream pipe size matter?

courses.title: Engineering Statistics
courses.summary: Data with judgment: probability, distributions, estimation, hypothesis tests, and regression — the quiet prerequisite under SPC, Weibull analysis, measurement uncertainty, and every reliability claim you will ever audit.
taught_from: MIT OCW 18.05 Introduction to Probability and Statistics (ocw.mit.edu)

lessons.scope: Location, spread, and shape; histograms and box plots that don't lie. Outliers treated as evidence, not embarrassment. Plant data pathologies previewed.
preview: Mean versus median on skewed repair times: which tells the truer story?
What does a box plot show that a bar of averages hides?

preview: Bayes in one line: why do false alarms dominate rare-event detection?
Independence: what does assuming it wrongly do to risk estimates?

What does the exponential's memoryless property claim — and when is it false?

courses.title: Electronics & Sensors
courses.summary: From signal to decision: analog conditioning, digital basics, and the industrial sensor families — temperature, pressure, flow, level, proximity, vibration — wired, scaled, and sanity-checked on a production line.

lessons.scope: Diode behavior and rectification; regulated DC supplies that feed every control panel. Ripple and regulation specs read from a datasheet. The 24 VDC world introduced.
preview: What does a rectifier-plus-regulator chain deliver, and what ripple means?
Why is 24 VDC the control-panel standard?

lessons.scope: The transistor as a switch; driving relays and indicator loads. Saturation, cutoff, and heat. Solid-state relays versus electromechanical ones.
preview: Transistor as a switch: what do saturation and cutoff correspond to?
When does a solid-state relay beat an electromechanical one?

lessons.scope: Amplification, filtering, and instrumentation amplifiers for small sensor signals. Common-mode rejection as noise defense. Why thermocouple microvolts survive a noisy plant at all.
preview: Why does a thermocouple signal need an instrumentation amplifier?
What does common-mode rejection actually reject?

lessons.scope: Logic levels, gates, and simple combinational thinking. Discrete inputs and outputs as the PLC sees them. Sinking versus sourcing — the wiring confusion resolved once.
preview: Sinking versus sourcing I/O: what actually differs in the wiring?
Why do PLCs read logic levels, not analog values, on discrete inputs?

lessons.scope: The industrial sensor families in one working tour — RTD/thermocouple, pressure transmitters, flow and level, proximity and photoelectric — each with its signal, failure modes, and wiring. The 4–20 mA loop derived as an engineering decision, live zero included. A filling line's sensor set walked end to end.
preview: A 4–20 mA transmitter spans 0–5 m of level and reads 12 mA: what is the level?
Why is the live zero at 4 mA instead of 0 mA?
RTD or thermocouple for a 90 °C bearing housing: which, and on what grounds?

lessons.scope: RTD classes and self-heating; thermocouple types, cold junctions, and extension wire sins. Thermowells and response lag. Choosing and installing temperature points that tell the truth.
preview: Why does an RTD self-heat, and how is it managed?
What does a thermowell cost you in response time?

lessons.t: Pressure and level measurement in depth
lessons.scope: Transmitter anatomy: sensing, isolation, and impulse lines. DP level measurement with wet legs — where the arithmetic goes wrong. Purges, seals, and Gulf-summer condensation traps.
preview: Wet leg versus dry leg in DP level: what changes in the arithmetic?
Why do impulse lines fail more than transmitters?

lessons.scope: Piezoelectric accelerometers with IEPE powering; mounting and its frequency ceiling, previewed for the condition-monitoring course. Eddy-current proximity probes for shaft motion on journal bearings. Tachometers and encoders — the speed and phase references every machinery diagnosis leans on.
preview: Why does an accelerometer's mounting method set its usable frequency range?
What does a proximity probe measure that a case-mounted accelerometer cannot?

lessons.scope: Sampling, aliasing, and resolution in bits. Anti-alias filtering as a non-negotiable. Why a vibration channel's sample rate sets what defects it can see — the data-collector connection.
preview: State the sampling theorem and one aliasing symptom on a trend.
What does bit resolution mean for a 4–20 mA channel?

lessons.scope: Grounding, shielding, and segregation of signal from power. Ground loops diagnosed and killed. HART over 4–20 mA in outline.
preview: What causes a ground loop, and what breaks it?
Why route signal and power cables apart?

lessons.scope: Specify the measurement points for a small process skid: variables, technologies, ranges, and installation details. Datasheets filled honestly. The exercise instrument engineers call 'doing the index'.
preview: What belongs on an instrument datasheet before purchase?
Pick measurement points for a small skid: what logic orders the list?

courses.career:
Electronics & Sensors is the reason the rest of the plant has numbers to control by — the sector picks which numbers:

  • Analog and mixed-signal semiconductors — component engineers at manufacturers such as Texas Instruments design the op-amp, regulator, and instrumentation-amplifier building blocks behind Lessons 1 and 3 — the same rectify/filter/regulate chain and CMRR-driven signal conditioning this course develops from first principles.
  • Precision sensors and data-acquisition ICs — applications engineers at manufacturers such as Analog Devices build the MEMS accelerometers and ADCs at the center of Lessons 8 and 9 — the mounting-resonance trade-off and the \( F_s>2f_{max} \) Nyquist criterion are exactly the specifications printed on those datasheets.
  • Industrial sensors and discrete automation components — applications engineers at manufacturers such as Omron build the proximity sensors and discrete I/O devices of Lesson 4 — the sourcing/sinking mismatch this course flags as the most common discrete-I/O wiring error is a routine technical-support call for them.

Interview angle: the classic probe is a diagnostic scenario — "this signal is noisy or wrong, walk me through how you would isolate the cause" — expecting a systematic elimination across wiring convention (sourcing/sinking), common-mode versus differential noise (CMRR and shielding), grounding (ground loops), and sampling (Nyquist and aliasing), rather than a guess at a single cause.

courses.title: Manufacturing Processes II — Machining & Joining
courses.summary: Chips and joints: machining theory and its operations, grinding, and the welding processes — the removal and joining half of the process world, with Kirby's fabrication lines as the standing example.
taught_from: Groover, Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing (Wiley) — machining & joining chapters
Kalpakjian & Schmid, Manufacturing Engineering and Technology (Pearson)
Black & Kohser, DeGarmo's Materials and Processes in Manufacturing (Wiley)

lessons.scope: Orthogonal cutting model: shear plane, chip formation, cutting forces, and the Merchant relation. Cutting temperature and where tool wear comes from. Taylor tool-life equation with its exponents read as economics.
preview: Cutting at 200 m/min gives 30 min of tool life; with Taylor exponent n = 0.25, evaluate the constant C = vTⁿ.
Where does most of the cutting energy end up, and why does that cap cutting speed?
If tool–chip friction falls, what does the Merchant relation predict for the shear angle and cutting force?

lessons.scope: Tool materials from HSS to coated carbides and ceramics; wear modes — flank, crater, notch. Insert geometry and grades read from a catalog. Cutting fluids and their delivery, with health notes.
preview: Flank wear versus crater wear: where does each live and what drives it?
What do coating layers on a carbide insert actually do?

lessons.scope: The core operations with their speeds-and-feeds arithmetic: material removal rate, machining time, and power. Workholding logic. One condensed worked example runs a job from print to cycle estimate.
preview: Compute MRR for turning at v = 150 m/min, f = 0.3 mm/rev, depth 3 mm.
How long is one 300 mm turning pass at 0.3 mm/rev and 800 rpm?
Why does chip evacuation dominate deep-hole drilling in a way milling never sees?

lessons.scope: Lathe and machining-center anatomy; axes and coordinate systems. What G-code programs actually command. Accuracy, repeatability, and backlash as machine-health quantities a maintenance engineer owns.
preview: What do accuracy, repeatability, and backlash each mean for a CNC axis?
Why does climb versus conventional milling change finish and force?

lessons.scope: Abrasive mechanics: grain depth of cut, specific energy, and why grinding burns parts when abused. Wheel designation decoded. Surface and cylindrical grinding with dressing and balancing practice.
preview: Why can grinding burn or crack a surface that turning would leave unharmed?
In a wheel marked A60KV, what do the A, the 60, and the K each specify?
What does dressing restore, and what workpiece symptom says it is overdue?

lessons.scope: Ra and Rz defined and measured; finish versus process capability charts. Residual stresses from machining and grinding. Why a ground bearing journal's finish is a reliability parameter.
preview: Ra versus Rz: what does each capture about a surface?
Why can grinding leave tensile residual stress, and who cares?

lessons.scope: EDM, wire EDM, ECM, laser and abrasive-waterjet cutting. Where conventional cutting cannot go: hardened dies, delicate profiles. Selection by material, feature, and tolerance.
preview: Why does EDM ignore hardness, and what does it demand instead?
Pick a process for a hardened die cavity's sharp internal corner.

lessons.scope: SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, GTAW, SAW: how each arc works, its consumables, positions, and typical defects. Heat input defined and computed. The process lineup on Kirby's structural fabrication floor.
preview: Compute heat input for 25 V, 200 A at 5 mm/s travel.
For structural work outdoors in wind, which arc process displaces GMAW and why?
Why is GTAW the default for open root passes despite its deposition rate?

lessons.t: Welding II, thermal cutting, and joint preparation
lessons.scope: Resistance spot and seam welding; oxyfuel, friction, and explosion welding in outline; brazing and soldering distinguished properly from welding. Thermal cutting — oxyfuel, plasma, and laser — with kerf and heat-affected edges. Joint types, edge preparation geometry, and fit-up as the quiet variable behind weld quality.
lessons.src: Groover, welding chapters; Kalpakjian & Schmid; Black & Kohser
preview: Resistance spot welding: what generates the heat, and where?
What does poor fit-up do to a weld's defect odds?

lessons.scope: Bolted joints: preload, torque–tension, and why gasketed flanges leak when torqued wrong. Riveting and adhesive bonding in outline. The maintenance view: joints are made to be remade.
preview: Why does bolt preload, not bolt strength, decide a joint's fatigue life?
What does a gasket actually seal against, mechanically?

lessons.scope: The AM process families in one pass: PBF, DED, FDM, binder jetting. Where AM already earns keep: jigs, obsolete spares, prototypes. Honest limits — surface, properties, qualification.
preview: Where does AM already earn its keep in a maintenance organization?
Why do printed load-bearing parts face a qualification problem?

videos.title: SME — machining and joining process videos
videos.note: Clearly narrated process explainers: machining, grinding, and welding — watch alongside each lesson.

videos.title: Manufacturing Processes II — IIT Kharagpur
videos.note: Machining-focused lecture series: cutting mechanics, tools, and grinding.

videos.title: Manufacturing lectures by Eng. Mahmoud Marzouk
videos.note: Arabic-language manufacturing-processes lectures (casting, forming, sheet metal). English sources above remain the primary path.
